title: Binary Search
prev: O(log n) - halve the search space

explain.desc: Binary Search works by halving the search space on every step. Compare the target against the middle element of the current range. If it matches, done. If the target is smaller, discard the right half. If larger, discard the left half. Each iteration eliminates 50% of remaining candidates. Only works on sorted arrays.
uc: Dictionary lookups
Finding a word in a sorted word list or autocomplete index.
Database indexes
B-tree range queries use binary search internally.
Git bisect
Finds which commit introduced a bug by binary searching history.
Standard libraries
Python bisect, Java Arrays.binarySearch, and Elixir Enum all use this.
Numeric approximation
Finding square roots or solving equations by bisection.

explain.desc: Bubble Sort makes repeated passes through the array, comparing adjacent pairs and swapping them if they are out of order. After each pass the largest unsorted element rises to its final position. The algorithm is stable (equal elements keep their order) and in-place, but O(n^2) average time makes it unsuitable for large datasets.
uc: Teaching
The clearest illustration of a comparison sort - ideal for learning.
Nearly sorted data
With early-exit, performs O(n) on almost-sorted input.
Tiny datasets
Acceptable for fewer than 20 elements where simplicity wins.
Embedded systems
When code size must be minimal and memory is very tight.

code.s: # Bubble Sort - O(n^2) time, O(1) space

code.r: (arr, i, m) when i >= m, do: arr

code.s: a = elem(arr, i); b = elem(arr, i+1)
arr = if a > b, do: swap(arr,i,i+1), else: arr

explain.desc: Dijkstra finds the shortest path from a source node to all others in a weighted graph. It works greedily - always expanding the nearest unvisited node via a min-heap. Stale entries in the priority queue are skipped when popped. Does not work with negative edge weights - use Bellman-Ford for those.
uc: GPS navigation
Google Maps and Waze use Dijkstra variants for route planning.
Network routing
OSPF protocol uses Dijkstra to build router forwarding tables.
Game pathfinding
NPC movement in games uses Dijkstra or its heuristic variant A*.
Social networks
Finding shortest connection between people (degrees of separation).

explain.desc: An AVL tree is a self-balancing Binary Search Tree. After every insert or delete it checks the balance factor (height difference between left and right subtrees) at each node. If any node has a balance factor outside {-1, 0, 1}, one of four rotations restores balance. This guarantees O(log n) operations at all times - unlike a plain BST which degrades to O(n) with sorted input.
uc: Database engines
Index structures need fast reads and writes - AVL guarantees both.
File systems
Directory trees and inode indexes use balanced BST variants.
Memory allocators
Tracking free memory blocks in size-ordered balanced trees.
Computational geometry
Range and interval trees are built on AVL-like structures.

explain.desc: TCP handshake establishes a reliable, ordered, full-duplex connection. Both parties must agree on Initial Sequence Numbers (ISN) - random values that prevent stale packets from a previous connection causing confusion. SYN sends the client ISN. SYN-ACK acknowledges it and sends the server ISN. ACK finalises. The 1.5-RTT cost is why connection reuse (Keep-Alive, HTTP/2, QUIC) matters at scale.
uc: HTTP/HTTPS
Every browser tab request starts with this handshake unless reusing a connection.
SSH sessions
Secure shell establishes TCP before the SSH layer negotiates.
Database connections
PostgreSQL, MySQL, and Redis clients all connect over TCP.
Microservices
REST and gRPC calls between services use TCP - connection pools amortise the cost.

title: HashMap Chaining
prev: O(1) avg - linked list per bucket

explain.desc: A HashMap stores key-value pairs using a hash function to map keys to bucket indexes. Chaining resolves collisions by keeping a linked list at each bucket. Average O(1) lookup because the hash function distributes keys evenly. Worst case O(n) when all keys collide into one bucket. Load factor determines when to resize.
uc: Caches
Fast key-based lookup in caches and session stores.
Database indexes
Hash indexes for exact-match queries in Postgres and MySQL.
Compilers
Symbol tables mapping variable names to memory addresses.
Routing tables
Web framework routers map URL patterns to handler functions.

code.s: case Enum.find(bucket, fn {k,_} -> k==key end) do

title: Quick Sort
prev: O(n log n) avg - divide and conquer

explain.desc: Quick Sort picks a pivot element and partitions the array into elements smaller than and larger than the pivot. It then recursively sorts each partition. Average O(n log n) with excellent cache performance. Worst case O(n^2) on already-sorted input with a bad pivot - fixed by randomised pivot selection.
uc: Standard libraries
Most language runtimes use introsort (quicksort + heapsort hybrid).
In-place sorting
When memory is tight - quicksort needs only O(log n) stack space.
Partial sorts
Finding the k-th smallest element via quickselect.
Database engines
Used in external sort operations for large result sets.

code.s: # Quick Sort - O(n log n) average

explain.desc: BFS explores a graph level by level, visiting all neighbours of the current node before moving deeper. It uses a queue (FIFO) to track nodes to visit. BFS guarantees the shortest path in unweighted graphs. All nodes at distance k are visited before any node at distance k+1.
uc: Shortest path
Finding shortest hops in unweighted graphs - social network distance.
Web crawlers
Crawling web pages level by level from a seed URL.
Peer-to-peer networks
Finding nearby nodes in distributed networks like BitTorrent.
Puzzle solving
Solving shortest-path puzzles like sliding tiles or mazes.

code.s: # Explore all neighbours before going deeper

code.r: (_, q, v, acc) when :queue.is_empty(q), do: Enum.reverse(acc)

explain.desc: An LRU Cache evicts the Least Recently Used item when full. It combines a HashMap for O(1) lookup with a doubly linked list for O(1) eviction. On every get or put, the accessed node moves to the front. The tail is always the least recently used item and is evicted first.
uc: CPU caches
L1/L2/L3 cache replacement policy in processors.
Browser cache
Recently visited pages stay cached; old ones are evicted.
Database buffer pool
Postgres and MySQL use LRU variants for page caching.
CDN edge nodes
Content delivery networks cache hot assets at edge servers.

code.s: # LRU Cache - O(1) get and put

code.r: (cap), do: %{cap: cap, map: %{}, head: nil, tail: nil}

title: Merge Sort
prev: O(n log n) - stable divide and conquer

explain.desc: Merge Sort divides the array in half recursively until each part has one element, then merges sorted halves back together. Unlike quicksort, it guarantees O(n log n) in all cases. It is stable - equal elements keep their original order. The trade-off is O(n) extra space for the merge step.
uc: External sorting
Sorting data too large for memory - merge works on disk chunks.
Linked lists
Preferred over quicksort for linked lists due to sequential access.
Counting inversions
Modified merge sort counts how unsorted an array is.
Parallel sorting
Merge is embarrassingly parallel - ideal for multi-core sorting.

code.s: # Merge Sort - O(n log n) guaranteed
# Stable sort - equal elements keep order

explain.desc: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It uses a stack (explicit or call stack) to track the current path. DFS is used for cycle detection, topological sorting, and finding connected components. Unlike BFS it does not guarantee the shortest path.
uc: Topological sort
Ordering tasks with dependencies - build systems, Makefiles.
Cycle detection
Detecting circular dependencies in package managers.
Maze generation
Random DFS creates mazes with a unique solution path.
Compiler analysis
Control flow graphs and dominator trees use DFS.

code.s: # Go as deep as possible before backtracking

code.r: (_, node, visited, acc) when MapSet.member?(visited, node)

title: Stack & Queue
prev: LIFO vs FIFO - fundamental data structures

explain.desc: A Stack (LIFO) adds and removes from the same end. A Queue (FIFO) adds at the back and removes from the front. Both achieve O(1) operations. In Elixir, stacks are plain lists (prepend = push). Queues use two lists - one for input, one for output - rotating when the output is empty.
uc: Call stack
Every function call pushes a frame; return pops it.
Undo/redo
Text editors push actions to a stack to support undo.
BFS/DFS
BFS uses a queue; DFS uses a stack - the only structural difference.
Expression parsing
Shunting-yard algorithm uses a stack to parse math expressions.
